title: The Nintendo Switch's days are numbered—but what is that number?
gist: Nintendo will stop selling the original Switch in Europe in February 2026 over EU replaceable-battery rules, but Ars's analysis of Nintendo earnings shows the 9-year-old Switch is unusually resilient—still selling ~13% of peak hardware and ~58% of peak software—with backward compatibility likely sustaining it into 2029-2030.

insights.claim: Nintendo will stop selling the original Switch in Europe in February 2026 because upcoming EU regulations require easily replaceable batteries in most consumer electronics, and Nintendo declined to redesign old Switch models to comply.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Concrete example of EU right-to-repair/battery rules shaping product lifecycle decisions; watch for similar regional discontinuations across electronics.
insights.quote: That decision comes in response to European regulations that will soon require easily replaceable batteries in most consumer electronics.

insights.claim: Nintendo is redesigning the Switch 2 to meet EU replaceable-battery rules while abandoning compliance for the original Switch, indicating it will invest in compliance only for current-generation hardware.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Signals how manufacturers triage regulatory-compliance spend toward flagship products at end-of-life transitions.
insights.quote: Rather than redesigning multiple original Switch models to comply with that regulation (as it is doing with the Switch 2)

insights.claim: In its ninth full fiscal year the Switch still shipped 3.8 million hardware units (about 13% of peak), versus the Wii which by year nine sold under 1% of its peak, marking the Switch as an outlier in late-life durability.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Unusually long hardware tail supports continued high-margin legacy revenue for Nintendo.
insights.quote: the Switch was still selling at about 13 percent of its peak annual sales in its ninth full fiscal year, shipping a significant 3.8 million units

insights.claim: Nintendo sold nearly 137 million pieces of Switch software last fiscal year, roughly 58% of its software peak, whereas the DS, 3DS, and Wii sold under 5% of peak at similar lifespan points.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Software attach rate, the higher-margin stream, remains extraordinarily strong deep into the platform's life.
insights.quote: Nintendo sold nearly 137 million pieces of Switch software in the last fiscal year, a number that's still roughly 58 percent of its annual software sales peak.
insights.locator: Things look even better for the Switch

insights.claim: Switch hardware peaked at nearly 29 million units in FY2021 and software peaked at over 235 million the following year, both higher than any modern Nintendo platform except the DS.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Establishes the enormous installed base underpinning the durable software tail.
insights.quote: The older system peaked at nearly 29 million worldwide hardware shipments in the 2021 fiscal year

insights.claim: The Switch 2 sold nearly 20 million units in its first partial fiscal year, heavily outpacing the ~4 million full-year sales of the original Switch in FY2026, suggesting large pent-up upgrade demand.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Strong Switch 2 launch de-risks the generational transition for Nintendo's hardware revenue.
insights.quote: the Switch 2 sold nearly 20 million units in its first (partial) fiscal year, heavily outpacing the nearly 4 million full-year sales for the Switch in the 2026 fiscal year
insights.locator: On the one hand, the Switch 2 sold

insights.claim: Original Switch software still outsells Switch 2 software nearly 3-to-1, showing the successor leans heavily on backward compatibility, which in turn keeps the older console commercially viable.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Backward compatibility is a double-edged lever, extending legacy-hardware life while driving Switch 2 adoption.
insights.quote: Switch software sales still far outpace those of its successor, at nearly 3 to 1.
insights.locator: On the other hand, Switch software sales

insights.claim: Developers are choosing to keep targeting the original Switch for games that don't need cutting-edge power (e.g., Nintendo's own Rhythm Heaven Groove), sustaining consumer demand for the older hardware.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Ongoing software support is the mechanism that keeps legacy hardware sales alive.
insights.quote: many developers are choosing to stick with the aging Switch as their platform of choice
insights.locator: Thus, for games that don't require

insights.claim: The $500 Switch 2 will soon cost about 63% more than the $340 original base console, making the older hardware an attractive value option and prolonging its sales.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: The wide price gap supports a durable low-end tier within Nintendo's lineup.
insights.quote: the $500 Switch 2 will soon cost about 63 percent more than the $340 original base console
insights.locator: Thus, for games that don't require

insights.claim: The author predicts many consumers will still buy new original Switch consoles into 2029 or 2030, even as the Switch 2 nears its fifth birthday.
insights.speaker: Kyle Orland
insights.so_what: Implies a multi-year overlap where Nintendo runs two hardware SKUs concurrently.
insights.quote: Don't be surprised if many stragglers are still picking up new Switch consoles into 2029 or 2030
insights.locator: All in all, while the Switch's best days
